title: Room Grammar — Charnel & Sanctum Encounter Bias
problem: BSP-generated rooms are geometrically varied but semantically identical — every room has the same encounter distribution regardless of shape. Long corridor rooms and wide open chambers roll the same odds. Players cannot read the dungeon before engaging it, removing a layer of spatial decision-making.

lineage.spawnReason: scope split: new dungeon intelligence layer not covered by boss-encounters — room shape now controls encounter bias

analysis: BSP dungeon generation produces rooms of varied geometry but uniform encounter distribution. Three room archetypes emerge naturally from shape metrics: Charnel (aspect ratio ≥ 2.5 — narrow corridors) biased toward elites and ancients; Sanctum (area ≥ 30 and ratio < 1.8 — wide squares) biased toward shrines and chests; Standard (everything else) unchanged. Classified at floor generation time, stored alongside rooms in gridStore, and applied as a weight multiplier before every encounter roll. Players learn the grammar in ~3 floors without a tutorial — narrow red-tinged passages signal danger, wide chambers signal reward.
implementation: grid.ts: added RoomType enum (Standard/Charnel/Sanctum), exported Room interface, classifyRooms(rooms) assigns type by aspect ratio and area, getRoomTypeAt(rooms, roomTypes, pos) looks up the type for any grid position. generateFloor() now returns roomTypes[] alongside rooms[]. gridStore: added rooms: Room[] and roomTypes: RoomType[] to GridState; initFloor populates both from generateFloor; movePlayer calls getRoomTypeAt(rooms, roomTypes, next) before rollEncounter and applies inline weight bias — Charnel: elite×3, ancient×2, shrine/chest≈0; Sanctum: shrine×5, chest×4, elite×0.3; Standard: unmodified floorPacingWeights.

decisions.decision: Classify rooms by geometry (aspect ratio + area) rather than random assignment
decisions.rationale: Seeded generation means room shapes are deterministic — classifying by geometry makes the grammar consistent and learnable. A Charnel room is always narrow; the player's visual read of the corridor predicts the encounter type.
alternativesConsidered: Random per-room type tag — breaks the visual grammar and makes the dungeon unreadable
Hand-placed special rooms — requires non-BSP generation and loses the procedural seed guarantee
decisions.recommendation: Geometry-based classification. Stack-agnostic: the same classifyRooms() function works on any rectangular room set regardless of rendering engine.

decisions.decision: Bias encounter weights, not override them — room type adjusts probabilities, not outcomes
decisions.rationale: A Charnel room that guarantees an elite every step would feel oppressive and exploit-prone. Biasing weights preserves variance and the seeded PRNG's run-reproducibility while still making the room type meaningful.
alternativesConsidered: Hard override (Charnel always spawns elite) — predictable to the point of feeling scripted
decisions.recommendation: Multiplicative weight adjustment inline in movePlayer. No new engine function needed.

successCriteria: Charnel rooms produce measurably higher elite/ancient encounter rates than Standard rooms at the same floor depth (verifiable via sim:floors). Sanctum rooms produce measurably higher shrine/chest rates. TypeScript 0 errors. Existing grid.test.ts and encounter.test.ts pass unchanged.
knownLimitations: Corridor tiles between rooms always resolve as Standard (no room boundary lookup for non-room floor tiles)
Room classification is purely geometric — no hand-authored control over special rooms
Visual distinction between room types not yet implemented (no floor tint difference)
userGuide:
The dungeon's room shapes are not decorative — they signal what's inside.

- **Charnel halls** (long, narrow rooms): high concentration of Elite and Ancient monsters. Move carefully, ensure potions are stocked before entering.
- **Sanctums** (wide, roughly square rooms): favour Shrines and Chests. Good rooms to detour toward when gear or buffs are needed.
- **Standard rooms**: normal encounter distribution — the baseline.

The grammar is deterministic per seed. If you die in a Charnel hall, the same hall will be dangerous on a repeated run with the same seed.

revisions.author: claude
revisions.reason: Initial full feature definition — Room Grammar implemented and verified

publicInterface.name: RoomType
publicInterface.description: Standard | Charnel | Sanctum — semantic identity of a BSP room

publicInterface.name: classifyRooms
publicInterface.description: Pure function: classifies each room by aspect ratio and area

publicInterface.name: getRoomTypeAt
publicInterface.description: Returns the room type at a grid position, or Standard if no match

publicInterface.name: GridState.rooms
publicInterface.description: BSP room list stored in gridStore, set on initFloor

publicInterface.name: GridState.roomTypes
publicInterface.description: Parallel array to rooms[], set on initFloor via classifyRooms

codeSnippets.label: Room classification logic
Encounter weight biasing by room type

implementationNotes: classifyRooms() is pure and deterministic given rooms[] — same floor seed always produces the same room types.
Encounter weight biasing lives entirely in gridStore.movePlayer, not in rollEncounter() — rollEncounter() signature is unchanged and remains unaware of room semantics.

Charnel threshold (ratio >= 2.5) was chosen to match BSP rooms that are clearly corridor-like vs. just slightly rectangular. Sanctum requires both large area AND squarish shape to avoid tagging large corridors as shrines.
roomTypes[] is a parallel array to rooms[] — indices must stay in sync. classifyRooms() is always called immediately after generateFloor() in initFloor.
